summary: The team connected checkout freezes to one overloaded checkout path that handled too many responsibilities.

messages.text: Database pool saturation lined up exactly with the checkout freeze.
Cart validation, inventory, payment confirmation, and delivery scheduling are all blocking each other.
This is now an architecture problem, not just an incident fix.

summary: Architecture participants agreed to split checkout into clearer order services rather than only scaling the existing monolith.

messages.text: Adding servers helps for a week. Boundaries fix the real problem.
Order creation should not own inventory reservation and delivery scheduling.

summary: The team aligned on order lifecycle events so StockSync and DeliveryTrack could update without blocking checkout.

messages.text: Delivery and inventory should consume events instead of being called inline from checkout.
StockSync needs order placed and inventory reserved events.
Then we need event lag dashboards from day one.

summary: StockSync stakeholders discussed temporary inventory reservations to avoid customers buying items that were already gone.

messages.text: The last carton of eggs cannot be sold to three carts at once.
Customers will accept substitutions better if we explain what happened.

summary: The team chose customer-friendly delivery statuses instead of exposing raw internal order states.

messages.text: Customers understand shopper picking and out for delivery. They do not understand internal flags.
Support does not need full driver location history for every question.

summary: Incident responders identified event retries without idempotency keys as the cause of repeated customer notifications.

messages.text: Orders are not duplicated. The notification event retried.
We need a stable key so repeated events are ignored.
Customers think we are sending multiple drivers.

summary: The team discussed freshness timestamps after stale store feeds caused bad search results and substitutions.

messages.text: Search needs to know whether the store feed is five minutes old or ninety minutes old.
Silent stale feeds need alerts.

summary: Security and product defined a safe support panel that avoids exposing unnecessary customer or driver data.

messages.text: Least privilege matters even for internal support tools.
Support needs delay reason and latest visible message, not every raw trace.

summary: The team agreed that order event changes needed governance because checkout, inventory, delivery, support, and analytics all depended on them.

messages.text: Order events are now a product contract.
Sensitive customer and driver fields need review before entering event streams.

summary: The group shaped EchoMind as a way to connect FreshCart incidents, decisions, tickets, PRs, and discussions.

messages.text: New people keep asking why checkout was split and who knows inventory reservations.
A graph makes sense because projects, incidents, decisions, and people are all connected.

summary: The team decided EchoMind needed a guided story layer before showing raw artifacts.

messages.text: If users do not understand the company story first, evidence cards feel random.
Memory Map should explain the system before people ask deep questions.
